common_name: Wild African Cucumber / African Horned Cucumber (wild)

planting.note: Sow seeds directly in a sunny, well-drained bed after all frost danger has passed and soil has warmed to at least 65°F. Grows rapidly in hot, dry conditions and tolerates drought once established.

directions: Sow seeds 1 inch deep in full sun after soil temperatures reach at least 65°F. This drought-tolerant scrambling vine needs little supplemental water once established but appreciates a deep soak at planting. Harvest the very young, pale-green fruits before they develop their characteristic spines and bitterness, as mature fruits require prolonged cooking or are best reserved for medicinal use. In cooler climates, start seeds indoors 3–4 weeks before the last frost and transplant after the weather settles.
sources: USDA ARS National Plant Germplasm System

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 4–6 weeks before the last frost date to give this long-season vine enough time to mature. Transplant to a permanent, frost-free location with a sturdy trellis or fence once nighttime temperatures stay reliably above 55°F.

directions: Start seeds indoors in warm, moist potting mix at least 4–6 weeks before the last expected frost, as cassabanana needs a very long growing season to produce its large, fragrant fruits. Provide a strong trellis, arbor, or fence — this vigorous vine can easily reach 20–30 feet in a single season. Water consistently and fertilize every few weeks with a balanced fertilizer; reduce watering once fruits begin to color and fill out. In frost-free zones (USDA 10–11) the vine is perennial and will produce fruit year after year from the same root system.
sources: USDA ARS National Plant Germplasm System

planting.note: Direct-sow after the last frost in a partially shaded spot with consistent moisture, such as a woodland edge or the base of a fence in dappled light. This native vine self-seeds freely and may naturalize in suitable gardens.

directions: Sow seeds shallowly in moist, well-amended soil in a partly shaded location after the last frost date. This delicate, tendriling vine climbs readily through neighboring shrubs, fences, or trellises and requires little care beyond consistent moisture. Harvest the tiny, striped-green fruits while they are still firm and cucumber-green — they are mild and crisp eaten fresh or pickled; never eat fruits that have turned black, as ripe fruits have a strong laxative effect. The plant often self-seeds generously, so allow a few mature fruits to drop if you want it to return next season.

planting.note: Sow seeds directly in early spring, 2–4 weeks before the last expected frost, as young plants tolerate light frost and benefit from a long, cool growing period. Inoculating seeds with a legume-specific rhizobium inoculant before planting improves nitrogen fixation and yields.

directions: Direct-sow lentils in early spring as soon as the soil is workable, planting seeds 1 inch deep in rows 12–18 inches apart; they germinate readily in cool soil and tolerate light frost. Choose a well-drained bed in full sun and avoid high-nitrogen soil, which promotes leafy growth at the expense of pods. Plants need little water once established and are highly drought-tolerant. Harvest when the lower pods turn tan and begin to rattle but before the stems dry completely; pull the entire plant and hang it upside down in a dry, airy space to finish curing before threshing.

planting.note: In mild-winter climates (zones 7–9), sow in early autumn for overwintering and spring bloom. In colder zones, direct-sow in earliest spring as soon as the soil can be worked, as seeds germinate well in cool conditions and seedlings tolerate frost.

directions: Sow seeds 1 inch deep in fall or early spring into well-drained soil; soaking seeds overnight before planting improves germination rates. Provide twiggy brush, netting, or a trellis for the tendriling stems to climb, or let the vine sprawl as a ground cover to suppress weeds. Tangier pea is highly attractive to bumblebees and other native pollinators and will fix substantial nitrogen when inoculated with a legume inoculant before sowing. Mow or turn the vines into the soil when flowering peaks or pods begin to set to capture maximum nitrogen benefit for the following crop.
